attributes.stem:
An 80-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with her daughter due to increasing weakness over the past few months. She has difficulty walking around her house and particularly struggles to stand up from her chair or bed. She does not become breathless on exertion and has no pain (including no joint pain) or recent weight loss. Her medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus and epilepsy. Her regular medications are metformin, sitagliptin, and sodium valproate. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally.

On examination, she appears frail but not acutely unwell. Her pulse rate is 72/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. She is able to walk with her four-wheeled walker and has a normal gait. She has normal upper limb power, but bilateral lower limb weakness with 4/5 power in her hip flexors and extensors. Her timed-up-and-go test is 17 seconds, with the predominant difficulty being rising from her chair. Initial investigations demonstrate:

  • Full blood count: normal

  • EUC: normal

  • LFT: normal

  • TSH: 2.5 mU/L (0.4-4.0)

  • CK: 100 U/L (30-200)

  • Vitamin D 75 nmol/L (50-150).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first step in management?
options: Advise her to purchase an electric recliner chair
Refer her for an Aged Care Assessment Team (ACAT) assessment
Discuss residential care options with her
Refer her for day rehabilitation or community geriatrics team
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with weakness that is particularly troublesome when standing up from the seated position, which is impairing her ability to function at home. Her examination findings are consistent with a proximal myopathy pattern. Common causes of proximal myopathy have been excluded on her blood tests - including thyroid dysfunction (normal TSH level), vitamin D deficiency (normal vitamin D level) and muscle damage (normal CK level).

The most appropriate first step in management is to formally assess mobility and transfers. The risk of falling is greatest when transferring, and therefore this is the initial focus in management. Specifically in her case, a timed up and go (TUG) represents a significant falls risk, and therefore organising an assessment of her transfers is the priority to ensure patient safety prior to implementing any other interventions or rehabilitation plans. This assessment can be completed by a therapist or doctor and will determine if she is safe to return home or if immediate interventions are needed.

Referring her for day rehabilitation or community geriatrics team would be an appropriate next step after ensuring she has safe transfers. These services can provide a comprehensive multidisciplinary approach including physiotherapy, occupational therapy, exercise physiology, and medical assessment to address her mobility issues and improve her functional capacity.

Advising her to purchase an electric recliner chair might enable her to stand up more easily, but it is a more expensive option and may result in further weakness if she stops using her muscles. It's not the most appropriate first step without a proper assessment of her needs and safety.

Referring for an Aged Care Assessment Team (ACAT) assessment or discussing residential care options are important considerations in the longer term, but they are not the most appropriate first steps. The priority is to assess and address her immediate safety and functional needs before considering significant changes to her living situation.

After ensuring safe transfers, the goals for her rehabilitation would likely include energy conservation techniques, improving strength and endurance through appropriate exercises, improving functional mobility with balance retraining, and maximizing independence in activities of daily living. These interventions would typically be implemented by a multidisciplinary team following a comprehensive assessment.
learningPoints: The risk of falling is greatest when transferring, and therefore this is the initial focus in management.
Assessing mobility and transfers is crucial for ensuring patient safety before implementing a comprehensive rehabilitation plan.
A multidisciplinary approach involving physiotherapy, occupational therapy, and medical assessment is key in managing mobility issues in older adults.
An older adult who takes ≥12 seconds to complete the timed up and go assessment (TUG) is at risk for falling.
references: Clegg A, Young J, Iliffe S, et al. Frailty in elderly people. Lancet 2013;381:752. Available at: https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lancet/article/PIIS0140-6736(12)62167-9/abstract
Podsiadlo D, Richardson S. The timed "Up & Go": a test of basic functional mobility for frail elderly persons. J Am Geriatr Soc 1991;39:142. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/1991946/

Schoene D, Wu SM, Mikolaizak AS, et al. Discriminative ability and predictive validity of the timed up and go test in identifying older people who fall: systematic review and meta-analysis. J Am Geriatr Soc 2013;61:202. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23350947/
Timed up and go (TUG) Assessment. In: CDC [online]. Available at: https://www.cdc.gov/steadi/media/pdfs/steadi-assessment-tug-508.pdf
